[08:00]
"To put that in perspective, that is their second largest acquisition behind Activision. The third acquisition is about $6 billion less than this. To put this into context, WhatsApp was purchased by Facebook for $22 billion. And in 2013, $13 billion buys you NASA."
[08:20]
"So that's a lot of money. And if you don't know anything about Bill Gates, Steve Ballmer, who is Bill Gates Microsoft CEO at one point, said the two most competitive people in the world he's ever met, Michael Jordan, Bill Gates."
[08:40]
"So they didn't acquire this for fun. This is a lot of money. Microsoft is trying to be the number one place in the cloud for all professional services, right? So collaboration, teams, all these things. And LinkedIn is their number one social platform for everything professional."

Like mine, I think last month dropped by like three or 400. Why? LinkedIn actively goes and deactivates dead accounts and accounts that look fake."
[11:00]

[11:20]

[15:10]
"A lot of times I'll reshare things. The company my company Kativ and Co State of Medtech is LinkedIn's customer. meaning no matter what the content is, no matter how good it is, they want me to pay them for more people to see that."
[15:30]
"But me as Omar, I'm LinkedIn's product. So when I post the same thing, I get so much more reach, so much more engagement. And LinkedIn encourages that because their algorithm is taking my content to figure out, hey, Omar knows his network better than we do."
[15:50]
"So this piece of content, oh, a bunch of people liked it from his network. Let's take that and go on an algorithmic journey and test it in front of multiple audiences to figure out what keeps them on. So they're constantly using my stuff to test. Make sense?"
[16:10]
"Small tactic you can do is that if you have a viral post on your personal LinkedIn profile, you can take a screenshot of that, promote it on your company page just as a picture and put a link to your profile so that way they go and engage with it."
[16:30]
"Post thought leadership content. So again, not IG flex post. There's nothing wrong every now and then put a flex post. That's perfectly fine. But there's some people from the inner circle that I've seen go on LinkedIn and they replicate exactly what they're doing on IG."
[16:50]
"Look on IG. Most of that shit is to impress like 18 to 25 year old boys for anybody who's under the age of 25. No offense, right? On LinkedIn, not only does it not impress people, it can work against you."
[17:10]

[17:30]
"but a huge amount of my content has gained a lot of trust and respect in my audience. So, when they see that, they're like, 'Oh, that's awesome.' Right? And the last thing is comment on post. I hate to say this, it's such a stupid cliche thing to say, okay, is social media. You're supposed to be social."

[22:25]
"Target audience. I have two messages I test. Just test two variables. They have to reply for information and then they get a video from me and then we book a meeting. Okay."
[22:40]
"very important you put thought into your audience. Do not do a mass thing like don't do you know like 10,000 people a thousand it's too broad. Okay you can use sales navigator and you can use things like annual revenue company headcount everything."
[23:00]
"if you don't use sales navigator. Sales navigator is a sales tool on LinkedIn. Use things like crunchbase. Do you guys know what crunchbase is? Crunchbase to show how much because here's the thing you want to know that the company you're trying to get in contact with has money."
[23:20]
"The dumbest thing I see people do in B2B selling is they go and they do like a mass invite. They get all these meetings and they're meetings with nobody's companies that barely raised any money. So, you want to try and pick your shots ahead of time and be very specific about where you're going to spend your time. Okay?"
[23:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Conference Targeting Strategy - Door Kicker Approach"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Floor plan de conferência, layout de expositores, comparação de tamanhos de booth, correlação com budget das empresas
[23:50]
"I like to use conferences. So, when a conference is coming up, it's great because it's an event with a date. It's very easy to personalize and make relevant. And then I use what's called a door kicker strategy that I learned from Jeremy."
[24:10]
"So when you go to a conference page, look up any conference coming up like CES, whatever. Look at what's called the exhibitor floor plan. This is a great way, and this isn't don't use this rule all the time. The bigger the space, the more money the company has."
[24:30]
"Not always applicable because for example, Medtronic doesn't have a huge base. They're about $110-$150 billion company. Globus is like a $20 billion company, but they got a bigger space. But for the most part, it gives you a good idea as to okay, here are the companies I can target and here's how I can do it."
[24:55]
"Question. Oh, I'll search the conference and what you can do is whatever the conference is like this is North American spine. I'll put NAS 2023 exhibitor prospectus. The exhibitor prospectus comes up or you put floor plan and it'll just, you know, you Google search it and they'll have the floor plan for you."
[25:20]
"Especially if it's a big conference. smaller medium-size conferences, they don't have it, but you can still just look up the sponsors and see like whoever the platinum sponsors are, like they got money, you know."
[25:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Door Kicker Strategy - Low Ticket Entry"
NÚMEROS: $1,000-$5,000 (faixa de cartão de crédito), $5,000-$10,000 (aprovação necessária), $10,000+ (deal longo)
VISUAL: Comparação de faixas de budget B2B, níveis de aprovação necessários, estratégia de entrada e expansão
[25:40]
"Door kicker strategy. Offer a lower ticket item to get into a whale account. The offer should be like an absolute no-brainer. I hate low ticket things. But if this gets you in as an approved vendor, do it because a lot of these companies, there's a whole process to become an approved vendor."
[26:00]
"So, when it comes to B2B budgets, it's going to vary. But from startups to enterprise, this is more or less how it works. $1,000 to $5,000 credit card, no problem. You know, no like, you know, a manager can do this."
[26:20]
"$5,000 to $10,000. Sometimes there's approval needed. For example, if I'm a director or a manager, maybe I got to get approval from my VP. If it's a VP, maybe they got to get approval from their CEO or something."
[26:35]
"$10,000 and above, it's a long deal. There's nothing wrong with doing those deals, but I'm telling you, if it's a company that's not publicly traded and they got like less than 500 employees, the CFO is going to be involved, the CEO, it's just too many things."
[26:55]
"You want to get in as quickly as possible and get them paying you and then land and expand. So, for me, I have an offer where it's a credit thing that usually companies have to spend 10 to $20,000 if not more. I'm offering it for like 3 to 5K, right?"
[27:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Outbound Sequence Timeline"
NÚMEROS: 2-3 dias entre mensagens
VISUAL: Timeline de sequência de mensagens, view de perfil + like de post inicial, 3 mensagens espaçadas, captura de email pessoal
[27:20]
"Here's a sequence that I do. First thing I have my thing do, go and view their profile and like one of their posts immediately because then that's going to get a notification. They have awareness. I've done something where there's a little dopamine hit."
[27:40]
"Right after that to get message one, I wait 2 to 3 days. Message two 2 to 3 days. Message 3 2 to 3 days. And after that I tell the sequence to end and get their email and again which is a personal email address."
[28:00]
"The reason why the personal email address is good is that if I go to their business account I'm competing with so many things in there. Personal email address really easy and they'll transfer me over to their larger email account. Okay."
[28:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Message #1 - Interest Hook"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Exemplo de primeira mensagem, oferta de "digital CME credit", estratégia de curiosidade, call to action para resposta
[28:25]
"First one is something that's going to get them their interest. I'm not going to read this whole thing, but essentially I say, 'Hey, I have this really interesting thing.' And I give it a different name. So, it's usually a CME credit. I say, 'I have a digital CME credit.' Something that's novel and different, right?"
[28:45]
"I tell them why it's important. Like, look, your sales can do product demos with this. It'll get traffic to the booth, etc. And then I say, you know, I can send you a short video on how it works. Do you want me to send it here or do you want me to email it to you? And if so, what's the best email?"
[29:05]
"The reason why you want this versus just sending the video, you want them to reply back, right? very important to get them to reply back because then you know where you should focus on that's telling you that there's an interest versus if I just send the video and I get all these views I mean some people might just look at it because they're curious right."
[29:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Message #2 - Detailed Process Graphic"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Gráfico de processo detalhado, estratégia visual impressionante, elementos de curiosidade (text/email CME, follow-up analysis)
[29:35]
"next thing if they don't respond to that I'll send another message but I want to show something more detailed again think thought leadership how can I impress them before the meeting so I'll send them this graphic where I was like hey this is the actual process and strategy of what we'll do I'd love to walk you through it okay."
[29:55]
"and I put things in here where they're going to say well how the hell does he do that like for example, text an email with a CME like, okay, how's he do that? Right? Or this, follow-up, or analysis, like, what does that mean? Right? Something that's going to impress them, something that's going to say like, yeah, I kind of want to know how he does that."
[30:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reply Rate Comparison: 1st vs 2nd Degree Connections"
NÚMEROS: 17% e 25% reply rate (conexões de primeiro grau), taxas menores para segundo grau
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado de resultados, demonstração da importância de conexões diretas, dados de duas conferências diferentes
[30:25]
"Now, here's a big insight that I got when I did this. On the right hand side, these are people who are my second degree connections, meaning that like we have a mutual connection, but I'm not directly connected with them. Over here is my first degree."
[30:45]
"The point that you need to get away from the slide is that people who are connected with you directly who have at least seen some content, they're more likely to reply. I mean, again, it's not it's not surprising to see that."
[31:00]
"In terms of the reply rates, these are two different conferences, 17 and 25% reply rate with first connections, which is good. So, the point is connect with all these people. Let them start seeing your content."
[31:20]
"So, when you do send something outbound, they've seen some stuff from you and they're like, 'Oh, yeah.' Like, versus connecting and immediately trying to sell, right? And then in terms of qualified deals, like the way it works is they reply, they it gets sent, there's a reply, and then I get them to qualify deal."
[31:40]
"Qualified deal for me is they say, 'Yes, I'm interested.' And in the video, I tell them how much it costs. I don't want to get a meeting and then in the meeting like, 'Oh, we don't have a budget.' If they're booking a meeting with me, they know how much it costs, right?"

[32:05]

[32:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Teleflex Case Study - One Deal into Multiple"
NÚMEROS: $9 bilhões (market cap Teleflex), $687 milhões (receita divisão 1), $322 milhões (receita divisão 2)
VISUAL: Estrutura corporativa da Teleflex, múltiplas divisões, demonstração de expansão de deal único em múltiplos
[32:30]
"First thing, learn how to turn one deal into multiple deals. Okay. What does that mean? Perfect example. And we might don't take a photo of this guys and post it. This is an active deal. So, Teleflex is a company that's got a $9 billion market cap."
[32:50]
"I turned one deal into two, okay? Where I I messaged with one person's division and then talked to them and I got introduced to another division. So, there's two deals now. One division does $687 million in revenue. The other one does $322 million in revenue."
[33:10]
"Why did I want those ones? This is something that I'm I've criticized myself about. I'm getting better about. I want to push you guys to do better. Sharpen your business swords. Buy this book. Warren Buffett accounting, learn how to read cash flow, balance sheet, an income statement, everything."
[33:30]
"because I went ahead of time because they're publicly traded and read these statements. So, I understood, oh, you know what? These divisions are the ones that are making money. This one's a dogshit one and I'm not going to go after because maybe they have money, but if I talk to the VP of marketing, they're like, oh, we're going to do this thing."
[33:50]
"Maybe the CEO is like, yeah, this this guy's doing a shit job. They haven't made us any money. No, we're not going to do that. Versus these other ones, they're making money. So, when they say, hey, we want to work with this company. we're going to allocate this much spend. It's like, yeah, sure. Go for it. Right."
[34:10]
"The more if the information is there, go get it so you know where to spend your time."
[34:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mapping Influence Beyond C-Suite"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Diagrama organizacional expandido, múltiplos níveis de influência, conexões entre marketing, vendas e C-suite
[34:25]
"Next thing, map your influence out beyond the C-suite. Buying decisions are more complicated. 10 years ago, even for ecom, it used to be that you can find one traction channel, put all your money there, and be done. Buyer journey is different."
[34:45]
"To buy a $10 product, you have to have different areas that you influence somebody, right? Same thing with B2B. Most people think of it like this. I'm going to go after the CEO and the VP of marketing, for example. Terrible idea. Dogshit right?"
[35:05]
"Next level beyond that, it's like, well, the VP of marketing has some people that report to them. I'll target those people, you know, influence them. That's a little bit better. The better idea is like, okay, if I'm selling them something that has to do with driving product adoption, making more money."
[35:25]

[35:45]
"but within the organization they've won presence club they have influence and the CEO loves them because they're like oh this this person's like a mini version of me and that person says you know I think we should work with Kativ and co right you have no idea where that influence is going to come from."

[36:10]

[36:25]
"So, I'm not going to leave anything up to chance. So, guess what I did as soon as I got the Teleflex deal on my calendar. I messaged one of their guys who I've been connecting with him, you know, and I haven't talked to him before."
[36:45]
"I sent him a message on LinkedIn said, 'Hey, Omar, you know, I have this meeting coming up on Monday. [37:25]
"And guess what? When I talk to him on Monday, I'm going to get every bit of information I can get so I understand what do I got to do to win this deal. I'm not walking in cold."
[37:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Turn Multiple Meetings into One"
NÚMEROS: 14 pessoas na reunião
VISUAL: Conceito de condensação de meetings, aceleração de deal velocity, eliminação de reuniões desnecessárias
[37:45]
"And finally, last thing, turn many meetings into one. The people who suck at B2B sales, have all these meetings because no one's going to criticize you for having activity, right? When I was running organizations, I used to look at sales, I'm like, you you've been meeting with this company multiple times."
[38:05]
"Turn three meetings into one. And the idea is that's how you accelerate deal velocity in a pipeline. Instead of having the intro meeting, then the presentation meeting, negotiation, do all that in one meeting and then do everything else through email."
[38:25]
"So, like Monday, I got 14 people showing up. I have 14 meetings showing up. 14 people showing up to this one meeting, right? So, I know exactly who I need to influence."
[38:40]
"And everybody there, like it could be a marketing associate who has no power, but who gets it. Their ability to persuade their VP to work with me is going to be better than me on my best day, right? You know, I have no more ego about this."
[39:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Everything is Negotiable - Payment Terms Example"

VISUAL: Screenshot de email de negociação, comparação de termos de pagamento, demonstração de flexibilidade de grandes empresas
[39:05]
"Very last thing and we'll get to Q&A. Once you're into these in into these companies, they're going to try and leverage you. Especially it's a huge company. Do not settle on their terms. Everything in Middle East I'm Middle Eastern. We have this saying everything is negotiable. Okay."
[39:25]
"Here is a big pharma company that I worked with. I got approved as a vendor with a low ticket item just to get in. Just to get in. This is like a month-long process. Okay. Big pharma will like audit the hell out of you."
[39:45]
"But here, this is their controller saying, 'Hey, you know, welcome. You got to set this up and everything.' Um, standard payment term is net 75. Net 75, meaning I'm gonna get paid in 75 days. That's insane."
[40:00]
"So, what do I do? Put your ego away. I send him this thing. I was like, 'Hey, you know, so and so, wow, that's the longest I've ever heard. That's kind of hard on a small business like mine. Can we can we be more fair and do net 30?"
[40:20]
"you know, I want to work with you guys, you know, but if you can't be flexible, I'll still work with you guys, you know, so that way I I took the pressure. I didn't say like, I'm not going to work with you. I say, look, this would be more fair to my business."
[40:35]
"And guess what? They say, 'Yeah, absolutely. We're going to we'll amend that contract. We'll scratch it. We'll do it 30, right?' So, everything is negotiable."
[40:50]
"All right. So, who has the first question? I got to format these things when I take them out of Canva."

[44:30]

[44:50]
"when you're a big business you've optimized so well that once you have money come in you can make a profit on that money even if it means that you they refund you back so for these businesses it's all about cash on hand."
[45:10]
"GE for example I just found out about this recently they'll do it like net 120 at the end of the quarter so if I do business with them at the beginning of the quarter, right? And I bill at the beginning of the quarter. Their billing cycle starts at the end of the quarter, then net 120."
[45:30]
"So, it's really important. And sometimes there's some businesses where you're doing so much volume, you have such good contracts, it's you you'll put up with that. But me personally, like that's not how I do business. And I try I don't allow people to do business like that with me."

[55:10]

[55:30]

[55:50]
"but then I can educate them and use things to kind of demonstrate my knowledge base and I say, 'Hey, you know, I know you guys are doing webinars and you're doing like why don't you do that with me?' In which case, that's like a $15 to $20,000 engagement, right?"
[56:10]
"And there's other ways to upsell them, right? And I think in terms of lifetime value, the way I see it is like how many ways can I help this person not just solve a problem but in these businesses like for example a marketing manager I'm thinking and I tell them this."
[56:30]
"I'm like look when you work with me I want to know like what are your wishes for the company? What are your wishes for yourself? Cuz now I know what's important to that person. I say hey look like I'll help you learn these things. I'll help you like you know because if you do XYZ this will put you in a great position to elevate your career into these kind of moves."
[56:50]
"Then when you do that, dude, people move around. They'll take you with you. And that's the best part of that I like about B2B is that these people move around. So like for example, I had somebody switch companies recently. Dude, that was a very easy vendor approval process."
[57:10]
"Cuz they're like, 'Yeah, I've worked with Omar before and we're going to work with him now.' You know, one of my mentors who's CEO that actually kept me afloat last year when I was building my course. I did like 30 or 40k just consulting here and there with him."
[57:30]
"He's going to a new company and he's like, 'Hey man, like can you help me with this company?' I'm like, 'Yeah, happy to.' Right. Very, very easy."
[57:45]

[58:00]
"Like for example, like this guy I'm going to talk to on Monday, I'm going to ask him like, 'What do you guys usually spend on things?' Like, 'Do you have an agency? What do you spend with them? What do they do for you?' I'm going to get as much information as possible."
[58:20]

[58:40]

[59:00]
"um and I might have like a better answer by that time. You mentioned the door is that also kind of flexible depending on whose door you're trying to get down?"
[59:15]
"oh totally so like for example like the people who are in the spine conference that door kicker I'm offering for like $5,200. The other ones, the cardiac one, I'm doing it for like $3,200 cuz I'm just trying to test price."
[59:35]
"And again, for that, it requires very little work on my end. Like it's literally a minute of work. So, I'm not going to get like greedy. I'm just trying to think like what's the number that people are like, 'Yep, do it.'"
[59:50]
"Because now, like that pharma company, I priced it perfectly. I priced it for like a thousand bucks. The lady did it like that day. And now that you know I did that little link for her, I followed up with her. I'm like, 'Hey, you know, do you have more events?' She's like, 'Yeah, we got like four more.'"
[60:10]
"And I was like, 'Great.' And I said, 'Hey, by the way, like, you know, I'm a small again, I'm a small business, you know, if you could help me in any way, I'd really appreciate it. Are there peers of yours or somebody else in the division?'"
[60:30]
"She sends an email immediately that day to a completely different division. This is a big pharma company. And the first sentence she says, she's like, 'Hey, this is Omar. By the way, you should use him. He's already an approved vendor.'"
[60:45]
"So some of these big businesses, it's a headache to get a vendor approved. It's a long process. So if somebody's already approved, it's like, 'Oh, I'll just use that.' You know?"
[61:00]
"You're like, you said door kicker. It's like offer, right? Yes. It's again, you're kicking the door and you can do this with whale accounts."
[61:10]
"So are you delivering on that yourself? Do you have a team member delivering upon that or like maybe is it like is it a service that you're delivering on?"
[61:25]
"It could be a service, it could be a product. For this, like I'm delivering it myself because again, part of what I'm selling is me, right? So, like if it's this credit, I go and, you know, I create the link. It just takes me two seconds and I send it to them."
[61:45]
"and that this link is for doctors to get continuing education credits. There's a report generated on my side, which is unheard of in the industry. So then I'll take that report and I'll interpret it and I'll go back a month later after their event is like, 'Hey, this is the report. Like here's some interesting findings and everything."
[62:05]
"like, have you guys considered doing this kind of webinar or this kind of strategy?' And that's where I kind of upsell them because they like I'm already it's almost like I'm getting paid just to get in and demonstrate my value versus what most people do is they do that for free in these random meetings trying to sell like a huge ticket item, which could work, right?"
[62:30]
"It could work. But like for example, my business is not at the stage that Chris's business is, right? So I need other tactics to get into these companies and land and expand."
[62:45]
"Smart type of client. Exactly. Exactly. And again, like one of the things I mean Chris is a big believer in it. same with me if you heard it in his talk is like I'm also trying to think like my time to value with my customers and what am I doing that's not only getting them results but like making the people making those decisions look really good."
[63:10]
"cuz if it's forget about publicly traded companies but even if smaller if it's a smaller company like a 100 person company but it's public or they're not those people have to report to a board so I want them to do something a lot of my stuff is radical for this industry so I want them to look really good to the board."
[63:30]
"So they're like, man, like the board knows my name because Omar Khateeb, like I'm going to do more stuff with him. See what I mean?"
[63:45]
"Sam. So I work at a management firm institutions. Facebook. Do you think LinkedIn ads could be something that we can test or what's your experience like you're trying to get investors, right?"
[64:05]
"Yeah. Yeah. So, I absolutely think so. LinkedIn ads, you got you got to have like balls of steel for them. The reason why is that it's very easy to spend a lot of money on there. But think about this."
[64:25]
"the ad strategies that no longer work on Facebook, they work on LinkedIn because a lot of these people, they're almost like new to social media. Some of them I'm making a huge assumption here, but a lot of there's people I know my age who have no social media, but they have LinkedIn, right?"
[64:45]
"So, absolutely, man. And the best thing about LinkedIn is that you could target you can tell them investor or VC or target these people in these groups, right? And what I would recommend is I mean I like King Keto strategy is really good, you know."
[65:05]
"because again what you're competing with on LinkedIn is all these terrible outdated strategies that B2B companies do like they're trying to do a lead gen to get a meeting. Dude, do a lead gen with some value adding book ebook or something, right?"
[65:25]
"And get those do a do a live webinar. If you guys do like live webinars, educating people as to what to do with your money, every week, every month, there's people with new money. Company got acquired, company went public, you know, your minimum investment is how much?"
[65:45]
"About a million bucks. About a million. Yeah, man. So, like all your all your people are on LinkedIn and so there's a lot of lot of CEOs."
[66:00]

[66:20]
"Like people who have been in my industry for a while and they're like, 'Yeah, actually like I don't know. I usually just go with what's standard with the company, you know."
[66:35]
"but with those live webinars you're doing, you're sending you're paying the platform to send outbound messages?"
[66:45]
"even better. I don't pay anything for those. So, what I do is I can I build my audience when I send invites out. There you can scroll and just select all and it'll just select everybody and sends them the invite, right? Then I can use Dripify and do outbound messages like that way."
[67:10]

[67:30]
"you know, so a lot of times what I'll do is like somebody who has 5,000, let's just say, let's say there's an investor that has like, you know, 2,000, you know, people in their network, not a lot. [67:50]
"Here's what you do. Click this invite button, scroll down until it's stopped and hit select all and invite everybody.' And now I just got this person's like, you know, like email list, right? Because they invited people who are not in my network but are in theirs."
[68:10]
"and I get all these personal emails and that's how I mean I'm the number one show in the industry mainly just from this tactic like that's what got me started earlier on."
[68:25]

LinkedIn, if you use a plugin, you know, sometimes you can get away with it because it's a Microsoft company, so like you can get away with a lot of stuff."
[68:55]
"Use a plugin, you're at risk where they might, you know, not ban your account, but like, you know, give it give it a suspension. If it's not a plugin, like dripify is not a plugin. Omni Creator, which is my software, not a plugin. It's totally okay."
[69:15]
"One thing, Sammy, remind me if you guys use Sales Navigator. Sales Navigator has great tools, more targeting tools and everything. Do not work within there. LinkedIn wants you to work within Sales Navigator because few years ago, Sales Navigator was 30 bucks a month. It's 90 bucks a month now."
[69:35]

[69:55]
"a app on LinkedIn. No, no, no. It's a separate company, right? Yeah. Yeah. You can when you go through the LinkedIn developer rules like they allow companies to make different verify."
[70:15]

[71:20]
"like hey you know I noticed you're going to NAS or whatever but then at the same time if you really want somebody's business you're like this is an important one I have a list where I'm like I'm not sending any automated I'm sending them personal message or I'm sending them like within LinkedIn you can click the video button and send them a video right."
[71:40]

[72:00]
"cuz when I actually reach out to myself like they're just going to ignore it you know so you want to be careful about those things and I think you get more with patience than with force there's some things that you want to go hard with other things you just got to be patient with you know."
[72:20]
"my recommendation to everybody do not do any because that's the easy part a monkey can get on and get dripify and start doing these automated things. Do the hard thing which is get on go through my course, build your profile and start putting out content."
[72:40]

They have a legit business. I've seen their stuff before.' You know, it makes it a lot easier."

[73:15]

[73:55]

[74:15]
"And then the following week, if I have a big show coming up or a product, guess what? That algorithm is going to be like, 'Man, his last post killed. let me put this new piece of content in front of a little bit more people."
[74:35]
"because always that algorithm the way it works which by the way the way the reason why I know this is because their engineering team publishes what the algorithm does and what they're testing on their engineering blog. I actually go and read it."
[74:55]
"So they're always taking the pieces of content and testing it. There's also hashtags on LinkedIn that have huge followings. [75:15]

"Connect with each other. If your first connections Exactly. your post is like starting to get traction, I get a lot of those notifications. People are commenting on Exactly."
[75:50]

Like a 9-month old post."

[85:20]
"like do things where you're like you know this this failed multiple times. I should post it dude I'm going to post that you know what a picture yourself. I'm not going to lie I'm going to post that this week it will blow up."
[85:40]

[86:00]
"And key hack here, sometimes I'll put out things like for I'll follow a CEO and there let's say they write about leadership or some topic. I'll go and write a post about that topic pretty much validating their point of view and I don't tag them, right?"
[86:20]
"Sometimes I'll tag them in a comment, you know, like, hey, you know, wonder what you think. A lot of times I'll send it to them and I'll say, hey, I'm, you know, your content inspire me. I wrote this post. No pressure. it would mean a lot if you can comment on my post with your thoughts because there's a lot of people in my network who I want to hear from you."
[86:45]
"So, think about that. I just created this imaginary thing where all these people are waiting for this person to make a comment and share their thoughts. Like, it's unbelievable how easy that works. I've had like really powerful people do that."
[87:00]
"DM people in my network say hey like I thought about you while I was making this video. Yes. And do not automate this. Yeah. Yeah. No."
[87:15]

[87:35]
"No, no, no. It just depends. By the way, you brought up something I want to comment cuz I was listening to some conversations last night. Here's something you guys This is like a for those of you who don't know, I'm married and I'm a dad. So, this is a very dad thing for me to say, but I had to learn I had to learn this the hard way."
[88:00]
"I'd rather you guys learn from my mistakes that people are not stupid, especially these big companies. They will look into you. And what you don't want to do is to think like, oh, if I make this post or I tweet this thing about this, you know, this political issue or red pill stuff or this and that everything."
[88:25]
"Yeah, it'll get a lot of engagement. You should always ask yourself, how the hell is this making me money? a lot of there's a lot of stuff that I'm very like passionate about and I'll tweet it or post it and I'm like is that gonna is that is that going to make me money."
[88:45]
"and then I think of like if the head of Stryker or J&J saw this like are they going to do business with me and the answer is I'm like no they're going to they might not like that and I made a post that bit me in the ass which it was 100% correct I'll tell the group here."
[89:05]
"it was a meme at the end of Pride month right because you know all these corporations they don't give a shit about people so at the end Pride Month, I put this meme where it's like like corporations on what is it like July 1st or like the day after Pride Month ends is like it's like all right back to normal like we love women, you know, like stuff like that."
[89:30]
"And a VP that I know who's a good friend and I would have never known this had he not text me. He's like, 'Hey man, like this me and it's a meme channel. It's not even my account. It's a meme channel, but people know I own it.' He's like, 'I got a message from one of the people in my group, you know, who's gay, who sent me a message into like, hey, is this the guy who's doing our training and sent me that?'"
[90:00]
"And I felt, dude, and again, it wasn't bad, but I felt one, I felt terrible cuz I made this person like feel shitty, right? That's number one. But number two, like that could have cost me my business. And keep in mind like that kind of thing. I deleted that. That was one of the only ones I deleted. This stuff can go around, right?"
[90:25]
"And so a lot of these people, especially if it's a publicly traded company, you better believe they're looking into that. So I'm not saying go back like a year or two and delete things. Don't worry about that. But just right now with a lot of stuff on your Instagram, on Twitter, if you go follow me on any channel, here's all you're going to see. Self-development. I read books. I love my family. I love my country. That's it."
[90:55]
"And I make mistakes all the time, but just really keep that in mind, right? Like you guys can make these posts on Instagram and a bunch of, you know, tweens are going to be engaged and everything. Those people aren't going to be the ones writing you checks. It's like, you know, older people."
[91:15]
"the tweens will be at some point do the Gary Vee thing. Like they'll be at some point like they'll have their own budgets, but just be positive, man. And I'm partly like talking to myself on this, you know? Any other question? I don't mind. I'm loving it. Nick, you can ask me a question, too, cuz Jeremy hasn't told me to stop yet."

[92:00]
"Yeah. Real quick, you know, with the nature of things being more valuable post and putting more effort in there, are you able to schedule or batch out? How are you doing with your internal ops to be able to push out this high level content, you know, on repetition?"
[92:20]
"Yeah. Yeah, that's a great question. So, part of the reason why I started that SaaS company. Um, what I try and do is I block out time on Friday, right? You know, to record my solo pod, to make the content and everything. I've hired a guy where again, does is the creative perfect the way I want every time? No. But it gets done and I pay him to also post for me, right?"
[92:50]
"And so a lot of times like you need just like data you need you know to answer Nick's question from you need volume to make decisions about what to do and even with the volume there's a lot of times where I'm like this is an outlier I think this can actually do better."
[93:10]
"and so what I would say is like batch content and when you get into a habit like I would you know I don't need to do it now but for you guys I would block time once a week to make content and schedule it on LinkedIn. use that software I told you guys to do that. And if you want book time with me, I could show you more."
[93:35]
"Um, and the other thing is put a five or 10 minute time block on your calendar every day to say get on LinkedIn and scroll and just go and engage and leave comments and everything, you know, and just think of it like this. You're not doing this for present day Chase, for example, or present day George."
[93:55]
"You're doing this for future Chase, future George, where you might be wanting to do an exit. You might have a shitload of cash and you want to do you want to jump in on some investments. Who in your LinkedIn network has been silently engaging and watching you that you've never done business with, but now you want to do business with."
[94:20]
"There's people who've been following me for years that I never thought that I want to do business with or need like all those VCs like I'm not raising money now. real easy for me to get them on my show, you know, talk to them even talk to them about like, hey, you know, I can't write big check sizes, but like do you mind if I like, you know, write a check into like one of your portfolio companies or something, right?"
[94:45]
"Like they're open to have that conversation. So, you just it's like that network. You don't know what it's going to do for you. Like you never know. It's really unbelievable."
[95:00]
"All right, guys. Give it up for Omar. All right. Thanks, guys."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$250K em receita de curso em 1 ano (Omar)
$40 milhões em receita (Chris, empresa iniciada em 2020)
$26.2 bilhões (aquisição LinkedIn pela Microsoft em 2015)
$22 bilhões (aquisição WhatsApp)
$13 bilhões (valor da NASA em 2013)
$1.6 bilhões (receita YouTube a cada 2 semanas)
$50-70 milhões (budget de marketing de executivos no LinkedIn)
$3 bilhões (ativos sob gestão de VCs no LinkedIn)
Quase 1 milhão de buscas no perfil
30,000+ seguidores no LinkedIn
12,000 inscritos no newsletter LinkedIn
4,000 inscritos na primeira hora do newsletter
17-25% taxa de resposta em outbound (conexões de 1º grau)
$1,000-$5,000 (faixa de cartão de crédito B2B)
$5,000-$10,000 (requer aprovação gerencial)
$10,000+ (deal longo, múltiplas aprovações)
$3,000-$5,200 (range de oferta door kicker)
$15,000-$20,000 (upsell de webinars)
Net 75 dias (termo de pagamento padrão big pharma)
Net 30 dias (termo negociado)
$687 milhões (receita divisão 1 Teleflex)
$322 milhões (receita divisão 2 Teleflex)
$9 bilhões (market cap Teleflex)
14 pessoas (reunião estratégica)
300 pessoas (participantes live stream)
45 pessoas (permaneceram após 1.5h de live)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Crescimento LinkedIn:
Profile persuasivo otimizado (upgrademylinkedin.com)
Company page real e profissional
Conteúdo thought leadership (não IG flex)
Comentários ativos em posts
Newsletter LinkedIn (mass invite automático)
Live events/webinars (captura emails pessoais)
Uso estratégico de hashtags relevantes
Omni Creator para agendamento e engajamento
B2B Outbound Sequence:
Plataforma Dripify para automação
View profile + like post inicial (dopamine hit)
Mensagem 1: Oferta curiosa com call to action
Espera 2-3 dias entre mensagens
Mensagem 2: Gráfico detalhado de processo
Mensagem 3: Follow-up
Captura de email pessoal
Priorizar conexões de 1º grau
Door Kicker Strategy:
Oferta low-ticket ($3-5K) para entrada
Aprovação como vendor aprovado
Land and expand com upsells
Targeting via conferências e floor plans
Foco em empresas com funding verificado (Crunchbase)
Deal Management:
Transformar 1 deal em múltiplos deals
Ler financial statements públicos
Mapear influência além do C-suite
Intelligence gathering pré-meeting (James Bond level)
Condensar múltiplos meetings em um
Negociar termos de pagamento
Content Strategy:
Batch content semanal (sextas)
5-10 min diários para engagement
Mix de formatos (vídeo, artigo, podcast, live)
Reutilizar conteúdo que performou
Pensar em 1 pessoa específica ao criar
Validar pontos de vista de executives
Evitar conteúdo político/controverso
ESTRUTURA:
Modelo de Negócio Omar:
Kativ & Co (consultoria medtech)
The State of Medtech (podcast/live show)
Omni Creator (SaaS - $20/mês)
Mini-curso LinkedIn gratuito
Serviços: CME credits, webinars, marketing
Flywheel de Crescimento:
Podcast/media atrai audiência
LinkedIn distribui conteúdo
Newsletter mantém engajamento
Outbound converte em clients
Clients viram case studies
Case studies atraem mais audiência
Hierarquia de Influência B2B:
CEO/C-Suite (topo)
VPs e Directors (meio)
Managers e Associates (base)
Sales reps com influência interna
Todos os departamentos relevantes
Aprovação de Vendors:
Processo de 1 mês (big pharma)
Auditoria completa
Uma vez aprovado = fácil expansão
Referências internas aceleram
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Pricing Tiers:
Door kicker: $3,000-$5,200
Webinar upsell: $15,000-$20,000
Curso online: $250K/ano de receita
B2B Budget Levels:
Até $5K: Decisão de manager/cartão
$5K-$10K: Aprovação VP necessária
$10K+: CFO/CEO envolvidos, ciclo longo
ROI LinkedIn:
Investimento: Tempo + $20/mês (Omni Creator)
Retorno: Deals de $10K-$40M
Half-life conteúdo: 6-9 meses (vs dias em outras plataformas)
Payment Terms:
Padrão big pharma: Net 75
Negociado: Net 30
GE: Net 120 (fim do quarter)
Market Size:
LinkedIn: 1B+ usuários profissionais
Medtech: Empresas de $110B-$150B
VC/PE: Firms com $3B+ sob gestão
DURAÇÃO: ~95 minutos FORMATO: Apresentação ao vivo + Q&A extensivo
